metadata.summary: ML-driven early warnings for schedule slip, cost overrun, resource contention, and price risk. Analytics pipeline feeds PM tools via alerts; humans retain all decision authority.

content.title: Predictive Analytics in Project Management
content.summary: Apply machine learning to historical project data to generate early-warning signals for schedule slip, budget overrun, resource contention, and material price risk — turning raw PM telemetry into probability-interval forecasts that change PMO decisions before events occur. The analytics layer lives in a separate ML pipeline feeding the PM tool through alerts and dashboards; humans retain all decision authority.

why.p: Reactive PM catches problems at post-mortem; predictive analytics catches them 2-6 weeks earlier. Studies show well-implemented ML-based schedule forecasting reduces cost overruns by ~15% on comparable portfolio programs. Reference-class forecasting (Flyvbjerg) is the required baseline — if ML cannot beat it, ML is not worth the data-plumbing cost.

list.item: Portfolio of 50+ comparable projects with 12+ months of clean historical data (issues, time logs, budget actuals)
Construction, engineering, manufacturing, or infrastructure programs where slip and overrun are repeat offenders
PMO needs early-warning dashboard and executive trend signals
Vendor/commodity-heavy programs where price volatility (steel, cloud spend) drives budget risk
EVM already in place and the org wants an ML complement for interval forecasting
Portfolio under 10 projects — sample size is too small; use heuristics + EVM instead
Brand-new project type with no historical analogues — no training data; use reference-class forecasting alone
Highly creative or one-off work (research, novel product) — past patterns do not generalize
Organisation without basic data hygiene (inconsistent statuses, missing actuals, freeform fields) — fix data first
Goal is optics ("look modern") rather than changing actual PMO decisions — model value requires action on output

section.name: ml-application-areas
section.p: Target use cases by ML type and PM domain:

rule.statement: Apply ML only where a PMO decision changes when the model output flips — define the trigger before training.
rule.rationale: A model that produces no decision change has zero value regardless of accuracy. Defining trigger first forces PMO alignment and constrains scope.

rule.statement: Use reference-class forecasting (Flyvbjerg) as the required baseline; beat it before claiming ML adds value.
rule.rationale: Simple comparable-project averages often outperform ML on small portfolios. The baseline prevents theater deployments.

list.item: Schedule forecasting: analyze past schedules, predict slip in days (regression); trigger: slip > 14d → escalate to steering.
Budget prediction: detect cost overrun early from EVM + vendor invoice patterns; 15% reduction in overruns documented in PMI AI reports.
Resource optimization: multi-variable allocation across concurrent projects; useful only when portfolio > 20 projects.
Risk detection: pattern recognition in issue velocity, comment cadence, owner-change count; feeds risk register.
Material price forecasting: Bayesian / interval models for volatile commodities (steel, cloud spend, semiconductors).

section.name: data-quality-requirements
section.p: Foundational requirements for model training and deployment.

rule.statement: Require clean, structured historical data with consistent status labels, dated transitions, and numeric actuals before training any model.
rule.rationale: Garbage-in/garbage-out dominates PM datasets. Most trackers have inconsistent labels, undated status changes, and missing actuals — no model survives that.

rule.statement: Predict intervals, not points: express schedule slip as "8 days ± 14 (P80)" rather than "8 days".
rule.rationale: Interval forecasts guide better decisions and communicate uncertainty honestly; point predictions create false precision.

rule.statement: Use GroupKFold cross-validation grouped by project or program — never random splits.
rule.rationale: Random splits leak future project data into past, producing optimistically biased evaluation metrics.

antipattern.reason: Survivorship bias: killed projects are excluded, making the training set systematically optimistic about completion outcomes.

antipattern.description: Shipping a model without a drift monitor.
antipattern.reason: Organisational change, new domains, and new tooling break models trained on prior projects within 6-18 months. Silent failure ensues.

section.name: human-in-the-loop-mandate
section.p: Decision authority, explainability, and governance requirements.

rule.statement: The model produces probability + explanation; a human retains all decision authority — no auto-action on budget or schedule.
rule.rationale: Decision-rights confusion is the top governance failure: agents schedule "auto re-baseline on slip prediction" when PM decisions belong to humans.

rule.statement: Ship SHAP values with every prediction; a black-box risk-register entry will be ignored.
rule.rationale: Explainability is non-optional in PM governance contexts. Stakeholder trust collapses on unexplained model outputs.

list.item: Mandatory human checkpoints: training-data contract approval, model promotion to prod, threshold changes, deprecation, any auto-action policy.
LLMs orchestrate — they draft data contracts, explain SHAP outputs, and propose features. They do not do numeric prediction.
Confabulated metrics: agents invent MAE/R²/AUC without running code; force metrics to come from sklearn output captured by a tool call.

section.name: pipeline-architecture
section.p: How to structure the ML analytics pipeline as an agentic system.

rule.statement: Treat the analytics layer as a separate ML pipeline (analytics/ repo) feeding the PM tool through alerts and dashboards — never as a Slackbot.
rule.rationale: Slackbot coupling creates brittle real-time dependencies; a pipeline with explicit data contracts and scheduled runs is testable and auditable.

section.p: A subagent owns the data contract (issue → feature row), trains and refreshes models, monitors drift, and writes predictions to a predictions/ datastore ingested by the PM tool via webhook.

section.name: recommended-subagents
section.p: Specialized ML orchestration and monitoring agents.

list.item: data-contract-agent (sonnet): inspects PM tool exports, defines stable feature columns, flags inconsistent statuses or missing fields.
feature-engineering-agent (sonnet): proposes engineered features (planned vs. actual ratios, queue ages, owner-change counts, comment cadence) with rationale.
model-training-agent (sonnet): wraps scikit-learn / XGBoost / LightGBM / Prophet pipelines; outputs model + metrics + SHAP explanations.
drift-monitor-agent (haiku): nightly — compares incoming feature distributions vs. training set; raises alerts when KS/PSI exceeds threshold.
prediction-explainer-agent (opus): turns SHAP outputs into stakeholder-readable risk-register entries.
password-scrubber-agent: scrubs PM exports before any third-party model call; Jira/Linear data leaks vendor names, prices, customer info.

section.reference: Baseline GBR regressor — beat this before claiming ML adds value
Feature contract schema emitted by the data-contract agent

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: Effective LLM prompts for data contract design and model explanation.

example.title: Data-contract agent prompt
example.note: Forces strict YAML output, explicit leakage exclusions:
example.code:
You are the data-contract agent. Inputs: 1. Sample of 5,000 issue rows from the source PM tool (CSV). 2. Project metadata (type, sponsor, budget, dates).
Emit STRICT YAML: features: - name: <snake_case> type: numeric|categorical|boolean|datetime source_field: <jira_field_id> nullable: true|false notes: "..." target: name: schedule_slip_days|budget_overrun_pct|risk_materialized type: regression|classification rationale: "..." exclusions: [ ... fields with PII/credentials ... ] data_quality_rules: - "status not in {Open, In Progress, Done} → flag" - "actual_start > actual_end → drop row"

example.title: Explanation prompt for SHAP → risk-register entry
example.code: Given SHAP values for prediction id=X (top 5 features by |contribution|), produce a 3-sentence English explanation suitable for a risk-register entry. Cite feature name, direction, and magnitude. Do not editorialize beyond data.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes in ML-driven PM analytics pipelines.

antipattern.description: LLM "predicts" budget overruns from prose narrative without running a model.
antipattern.reason: LLMs are not numeric forecasters. They confabulate plausible-sounding numbers. Train a model; LLMs draft, explain, and orchestrate.

antipattern.description: Random train/test split used for project data.
antipattern.reason: Random splits leak future project data into past training. Group splits by project_id or program are mandatory.

antipattern.description: Agent declares "deployed" after model.fit() without wiring a drift monitor.
antipattern.reason: Shipping a model without monitoring is silent failure. Force a monitoring task into the SDD chain before calling any deployment complete.

antipattern.description: Sending raw PM CSV rows to a third-party LLM API.
antipattern.reason: PM data contains salary, vendor pricing, customer names. Tokenize or run on-prem; never send raw rows externally.

list.item: Data leakage: agents conflate target and feature when columns share names (e.g., `final_cost` vs. `forecast_cost`). Force a leakage audit step.
Sample-size collapse: small samples + many features → overfitting. Force a feature-count cap (e.g., features ≤ N/10).
Bias replication: protected-class data (region, business-unit reputation) leaks into "high-risk" labels. Run fairness checks; remove proxies.
Drift-detection thresholds hallucinated: agents pick "reasonable" KS thresholds that fire constantly or never. Calibrate against a validation period.
Long-context drift: LLMs lose the schema after ~3,000 lines of CSV. Always operate on the data contract YAML, not raw rows.
Cost runaway: hyperparameter search via cloud GPUs without a budget cap. Cap with explicit budget guardrails per training run.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Python packages and utilities for feature engineering, modeling, and monitoring.

list.item: pandas / polars — feature engineering, ETL of PM data (pip install pandas polars)
scikit-learn / xgboost / lightgbm — tabular ML for slip/overrun/risk (pip install scikit-learn xgboost lightgbm)
prophet / sktime / darts — time-series for budget runs and material prices (pip install prophet sktime darts)
shap / lime — model explanation; required for governance (pip install shap lime)
evidently / nannyml / whylogs — drift and data-quality monitoring (pip install evidently nannyml whylogs)
mlflow / wandb / dvc — experiment tracking and model registry (pip install mlflow wandb dvc)
dbt — SQL transformations of PM warehouse data into feature tables (https://docs.getdbt.com)
airbyte / fivetran — source connectors for Jira, Asana, ClickUp, Salesforce (https://airbyte.com / https://fivetran.com)
duckdb — local OLAP on exported CSVs without a warehouse (pip install duckdb)
feast — feature store for online + offline parity (https://feast.dev)
great-expectations — data-quality assertions in CI (https://greatexpectations.io)
prefect / dagster / airflow — orchestrate ETL → train → score → publish pipeline

section.name: services-and-saas
section.p: Platform and service options for PM analytics.

list.item: Atlassian Intelligence (Jira) — LLM-summarization features; minimal predictive ML at present.
ClickUp AI / Asana Intelligence / Monday AI — mostly auto-summary and field generation; predictive features shallow.
Microsoft Project Cortex / Viva Insights — resource and meeting analytics; useful for capacity inputs.
Power BI + Project for the Web — cheapest enterprise-grade visualization on Microsoft estate.
Tableau / Looker / Sigma — common dashboarding for PM analytics; pair with warehouse.
Snowflake / BigQuery / Databricks / Redshift — warehouse for unified PM data; required at scale.
dbt Cloud — transformation layer between sources and ML.
Procore + Procore Analytics — construction-specific PM with built-in analytics.
Autodesk Construction Cloud (BIM 360) — construction analytics + risk insights.
Oracle Primavera + Risk Analysis — heavy predictive scheduling for capital projects (Monte Carlo).
Deltek Cobra / Acumen — EVM + risk analytics for govt / aerospace contracts.
nPlan / ALICE Technologies — ML-driven schedule risk and AI scheduling for construction.
KEEL Solutions / Disperse / OpenSpace — construction site progress capture feeding analytics.
AlphaSense / Bloomberg Terminal / S&P Capital IQ — material price and macro forecasting feeds.
Hugging Face / Anthropic / OpenAI / Vertex AI / Azure ML — LLM/ML hosts; route via privacy DPA + zero-retention only.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Proven patterns for successful PM analytics deployments.

rule.statement: Start with one decision: the model only matters if a PMO action changes when its output flips. Define the trigger before training.
rule.rationale: Model value requires decision change. Defining the trigger first forces PMO alignment and constrains scope to the achievable.

rule.statement: Reference-class forecasting (Flyvbjerg) as a baseline: simple comparable-project averages often beat ML on small portfolios. Beat the baseline before going complex.
rule.rationale: ML complexity is not justified if simple reference classes perform equally. The baseline prevents premature optimization.

rule.statement: Calibrate probabilities (Platt / isotonic) before publishing — uncalibrated risk scores produce false alarms.
rule.rationale: Raw sigmoid outputs from gradient boosters are not calibrated probabilities; stakeholders treating them as such will make misinformed decisions.

rule.statement: Group splits in cross-validation by project or program — random splits leak future into past.
rule.rationale: Proper fold design prevents optimistically biased evaluation metrics that hide real-world performance degradation.

rule.statement: Explainability is non-optional in PM. Ship SHAP values with every prediction; a black-box risk-register entry will be ignored.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder trust requires transparent reasoning. Black-box outputs have zero governance value in PM contexts.

rule.statement: Drift-monitor in production: KS / PSI on input distributions, calibration drift on outputs, monthly retrains as default.
rule.rationale: Models degrade silently without monitoring. Detecting and responding to drift early prevents organizational surprise.

rule.statement: Keep humans in the loop for any escalation. Auto-action is reserved for low-stakes nudges (assignee reminders), never for budget or schedule changes.
rule.rationale: Decision authority belongs to humans. Predictions inform; decisions belong to organizational leadership.

rule.statement: Version the data, not just the model. Use DVC or warehouse snapshots; "model says X" is meaningless without "trained on snapshot Y".
rule.rationale: Without data versioning, debugging model degradation is impossible; data provenance is as critical as model reproducibility.

rule.statement: Document model cards (intended use, training data, metrics, limitations) per Mitchell et al. — required by AI Act for high-risk systems.
rule.rationale: Model cards make assumptions explicit, preventing misapplication and satisfying emerging regulatory requirements.

rule.statement: Cap PII / financial data scope to what the model needs; tokenize or hash identifiers.
rule.rationale: Minimizing sensitive data exposure reduces privacy and compliance risk without sacrificing model performance.

rule.statement: Pair with `risk-management/`: model outputs are inputs to the register, not replacements.
rule.rationale: ML predictions augment but do not replace formal risk management processes; integration is bidirectional.

section.name: where-it-fails
section.p: Known limitations and common failure patterns.

list.item: Garbage-in/garbage-out dominates. Most PM trackers have inconsistent labels, undated status changes, and missing actuals — no model survives that.
Concept drift: organisational change, new domains, new tooling all break models trained on prior projects within 6–18 months.
Predictions become self-fulfilling or self-defeating: if the PMO acts on "delay predicted", the prediction may never realize, making model evaluation hard.
Black-box models lose stakeholder trust fast in PM contexts. Explainability (SHAP, LIME, partial dependence) is mandatory for governance.
Model bias replicates historical inequities — projects from disfavored business units may be systematically labeled "high-risk".
Material price models (steel, copper, semis) inherit macro-volatility; Bayesian / interval forecasts beat point predictions.
Resource optimization models often produce mathematically optimal but politically infeasible allocations.
Many vendor "AI PM" features are LLM auto-summarizers, not predictive analytics — verify before integrating.

template.purpose: Baseline GBR schedule-slip regressor (scikit-learn, GroupKFold, SHAP-ready)
Feature contract schema for the data-contract agent
